content:
**k-Nearest Neighbors** grew from **nonparametric statistics** and **pattern recognition** before it was branded as machine learning. **Fix** and **Hodges** (1951) proved **asymptotic consistency** of the **1-NN rule** — as sample size grows, error approaches twice the Bayes optimal rate under mild conditions — establishing k-NN as a theoretical benchmark.

**Cover** and **Hart** (1967) analyzed **k-NN** with general **k**, linking neighbor count to **risk** and **smoothness** of the decision rule.

Early **pattern recognition** systems (1960s–70s) used **template matching** and **nearest prototype** rules — practical ancestors of modern k-NN on feature vectors.

**Devroye** and others (1980s–90s) sharpened **finite-sample** and **high-dimensional** behavior, including distance concentration effects relevant today.

**Cover**'s **universal approximation** intuition and k-NN's role as a **baseline classifier** carried into **data mining** (1990s) and **recommender** neighborhood methods.

With **big data**, exact k-NN at billion-point scale drove **approximate nearest neighbor** research (LSH, product quantization, HNSW) while the core **vote among k closest stored examples** algorithm remains unchanged.

timeline.label: 1-NN consistency (1951)
timeline.description: Fix and Hodges show 1-NN is consistent as n → ∞ — foundation for instance-based learning theory.

timeline.label: General k-NN analysis (1967)
timeline.description: Cover and Hart formalize k-NN risk and the role of k in smoothing the decision rule.

timeline.label: Pattern recognition era (1960s–70s)
timeline.description: Nearest-neighbor and template classifiers deployed on early feature-based recognition tasks.

timeline.label: Nonparametric ML theory (1980s–90s)
timeline.description: Finite-sample bounds and high-dimensional limitations refine when k-NN works well.

timeline.label: Scalable ANN search (2000s–present)
timeline.description: FAISS, Annoy, HNSW, and LSH make neighbor search practical at web scale while preserving the k-NN rule.
